metadata.verificationStatus: COMPLETE - All 167 practices verified and extracted

controls.processArea: Asset Integrity and Process Safety Management
controls.controlName: Establish HAZOP Schedule and Resource Allocation
controls.controlStatement: Who: HEMP Process Owner | What: Establish and maintain a schedule for 5-year HAZOP studies with allocated resources and budget | When: Annually; schedule updated within 60 days of previous HAZOP completion | Where: Asset HAZOP plan document, visible on HEMP dashboard | Why: To ensure HAZOP studies meet 5-year revalidation requirement and prevent gaps in hazard identification | How: Document schedule showing unit names, HAZOP approach, previous completion dates, planned dates, resource requirements; obtain leadership approval; track against milestones | Evidence: Signed Asset HAZOP Plan with resource approval, timeline tracking log, quarterly leadership review sign-offs
keyActivities: Compile list of all units with RAM Red or Yellow 5A/5B process safety risks
Identify units due for HAZOP review within next 12 months
Calculate study duration and budget for each HAZOP
Schedule HAZOP workshops with buffer time before 5-year deadline
Allocate competent HAZOP facilitators and team members
Obtain leadership commitment and funding approval
Document plan with completion dates and review schedule
controls.riskAddressed: Delays or missed 5-year HAZOP cycles leading to unidentified hazards and process safety gaps; loss of institutional knowledge on hazard control strategies
controls.testingApproach: Review HAZOP Plan document; verify all units with RAM Red/Yellow 5A/5B risks are listed; check that next HAZOP completion date is within 5 years of last completion; confirm resource allocation and budget; interview HEMP Process Owner on tracking mechanism

controls.processArea: Asset Integrity and Process Safety Management
controls.controlName: Technical Information Gathering for HAZOP Scope
controls.controlStatement: Who: HAZOP Facilitator and HEMP Focal Point | What: Compile and verify current technical information including MOC documents, maintenance history, operating window data, and SIF performance records before finalizing HAZOP scope | When: 6-9 months before planned HAZOP workshop | Where: Technical file or HAZOP preparation folder; referenced in Terms of Reference | Why: Ensures HAZOP team has complete, accurate data to assess barrier validity and identify scope gaps; reveals trends in equipment reliability and operational exceedances | How: Gather MOCs since last HAZOP, review Design Operating Window and Integrity Operating Window exceedances, analyze PM/CM backlog status, extract SIF probability of failure data, compile assurance findings; conduct technical gap review | Evidence: Checklist showing all required documents collected, MOC summary log, maintenance status report, SIF performance data, assurance finding summary

controls.processArea: Asset Integrity and Process Safety Management
controls.controlName: HAZOP Study Approach Selection and Documentation
controls.controlStatement: Who: HAZOP Facilitator with Technical Safety TA2 or Business-Approved HEMP SME and Asset Operations/Engineering representatives | What: Evaluate and select appropriate HAZOP approach (new, targeted update, or full update) based on documented criteria and record decision with technical rationale | When: 9-12 months before HAZOP workshop; decision finalized 60 days before commencement | Where: HAZOP Terms of Reference; documented in HEMP Plan approval record | Why: Prevents wasted effort on redundant studies while ensuring scope adequacy; directs appropriate level of scrutiny to high-risk or changed areas | How: Apply evaluation criteria from Appendix B (previous HAZOP robustness, MOC process effectiveness, incident learning, barrier validity); assess equipment condition and performance trends; document selection decision with justification; obtain approvals from authorized signatories | Evidence: Signed HAZOP TOR with approach selection and rationale clearly stated; evaluation criteria worksheet; management approval email or document

controls.processArea: Asset Integrity and Process Safety Management
controls.controlName: Field Verification Walk-Through and Technical Assessment
controls.controlStatement: Who: HAZOP team members including process engineer, operations representative, and maintenance specialist | What: Conduct guided walk-through of unit before and after HAZOP workshop to verify field conditions match assumptions and identify discrepancies | When: Before HAZOP workshop (planning walk-through) and within 2 weeks after workshop completion | Where: Operating unit; findings documented on technical field review checklist | Why: Validates HAZOP assumptions against actual field conditions; identifies barriers that are missing, degraded, or mislocated; catches scenarios missed in workshop analysis | How: Use HSSE Technical Field Review Checklist from Appendix C; focus on barrier availability, equipment condition, safeguarding installation, accessibility, fire protection, and operational integrity; photograph critical items; record discrepancies and actions taken | Evidence: Completed HSSE Technical Field Review Checklist with date and signatures; photographic record of critical barriers; field walk-through report with findings and resolution actions

controls.processArea: Asset Integrity and Process Safety Management
controls.controlName: Communication of HAZOP Results to Frontline and Management
controls.controlStatement: Who: HEMP Process Owner and Asset Leadership | What: Communicate HAZOP findings, recommendations, and critical scenarios to operations, maintenance, and affected employees within 30 days of completion; include interim controls and resource requirements | When: Within 30 days of HAZOP completion; before implementation of major actions | Where: Toolbox talks, team meetings, safety bulletins, shift briefings; documented in meeting records | Why: Ensures frontline understands hazard scenarios and barriers; builds ownership of controls; highlights interim measures and resource dependencies | How: Organize feedback sessions with operations and maintenance teams; highlight HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers; present key recommendations and action priorities; distribute one-page summaries; conduct toolbox talks on high-impact scenarios | Evidence: Meeting attendance records and sign-in sheets; summary documents distributed; team acknowledgment forms; shift brief records referencing HAZOP findings

controls.processArea: Asset Integrity and Process Safety Management
controls.controlName: Annual Leadership Review of HAZOP and Action Status
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Leadership (Operations Manager, Technical Authority) | What: Conduct formal annual review of HAZOP plan, action closure status, and compliance with 5-year revalidation requirement; assess resource adequacy | When: Annually, typically in Q4 for the following year planning | Where: Leadership meeting; HAZOP Plan visible management review document | Why: Maintains leadership awareness and commitment; identifies resource gaps or schedule risks; ensures actions from previous HAZOPs are progressing toward closure | How: Review HAZOP Plan showing all pending actions; verify action closure with supporting evidence; assess adequacy of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barrier maintenance; confirm next HAZOP schedule meets 5-year requirement; discuss resource constraints; commit to required funding | Evidence: Signed annual leadership HAZOP review record; updated HAZOP Plan with action status; resource allocation decision memo; Q4 leadership meeting minutes

controls.subProcess: Confined Space Isolation and Entry Preparation
controls.controlName: Positive Isolation of Confined Space from Hazard Sources
controls.controlStatement: Who: Permit Authoriser and Isolation Technician (licensed) | What: Install and verify positive isolation (blind or air gap) on all sources of hazardous substances or energy connected to confined space; isolate as close to space as possible | When: Before confined space entry permit is issued; isolation tested and verified before entry | Where: Equipment inlet/outlet connections, isolation points documented on P&ID or isolation checklist | Why: Prevents ingress of hazardous materials or energy into confined space during entry; protects entrants from external process hazards; required barrier for safe entry | How: Use Safe Isolation Hierarchy (air gap preferred, then blind, spectacle blind, or spade); install isolation device; apply lockout tags; confirm isolation on checklist; witness verification by second person; sign off on permit | Evidence: Completed Safe Isolation Certificate with date, installer, and verifier signatures; photographic proof of isolation installation; checklist showing each isolation point confirmed; permit annotation

controls.subProcess: Confined Space Isolation and Entry Preparation
controls.controlName: Removal of Ionizing Radiation Sources Before Entry
controls.controlStatement: Who: Authorized Person for Ionizing Radiation (certified) | What: Remove or secure radioactive sources (level gauges, sources on vessels) and lock in shielded container or shut shutter before confined space entry begins | When: As part of pre-entry hazard freeing; before atmospheric testing starts | Where: Location of radiological source; documented on entry permit | Why: Prevents acute radiation exposure to entrants; NORM (Naturally Occurring Radioactive Material) can cause serious health effects in confined space; required to establish safe atmospheric conditions | How: Identify all radiological sources on or connected to equipment; verify with radiation safety specialist; remove source to shielded storage or activate shutter with witness verification; document removal or securement on pre-entry checklist | Evidence: Radiological source removal record with date and authorizer signature; secured/shuttered source verification photo; radiation safety officer sign-off; permit annotation

controls.controlName: Pre-Entry Atmospheric Testing Per ANSI Z117.1 Limits
controls.controlStatement: Who: Authorized Gas Tester (trained and certified) | What: Test confined space atmosphere for oxygen, flammability, and toxicity before entry permit is issued; record results on permit; repeat at specified frequency during work | When: Initial test within 4 hours before permit issue; periodic testing per frequency specified on permit (minimum every 2 hours unless continuous monitoring) | Where: Multiple points throughout space including high and low points; documented with test location notes on atmospheric testing section of permit | Why: Verifies atmosphere is safe for human breathing and prevents fire/explosion hazard; oxygen depletion, toxic gas accumulation, or flammable atmosphere would cause rapid entrant incapacitation or death | How: Use calibrated multi-gas detector (explosion-proof rating); test at least 3 locations (top, middle, bottom); record O2%, LEL%, and toxicant ppm; ensure readings fall within safe bands: O2 19.5-23.5%, LEL ≤10%, toxins ≤published limits; mark test time, detector type, and AGT initials on permit | Evidence: Completed atmospheric testing record on permit showing multiple test points and times; gas detector calibration certificate; AGT name and signature on permit; continuous monitoring logs if applicable

controls.controlName: Continuous Atmospheric Monitoring During Entry
controls.controlStatement: Who: Authorized Gas Tester (stationed outside space) and Confined Space Entry Attendant | What: Maintain continuous atmospheric monitoring with calibrated detector connected to entrants via cable; immediately alert if levels drift outside safe parameters; cease work if alarm activates | When: Continuously during any entry into space | Where: Detector held/carried outside space by AGT or Entry Attendant; monitor display visible to both AGT and supervisor | Why: Detects atmospheric changes caused by process ingress, ventilation failure, or chemical reactions; provides early warning to evacuate before incapacitation occurs; prevents fatalities from undetected gas presence | How: Deploy gas detector before entrants descend; keep detector operational entire duration of entry; ensure alarm set at safe thresholds; brief entrants on alarm signal and evacuation procedure; have backup detector available; test detector function at start and during entry | Evidence: Continuous atmospheric monitoring log documenting monitor operation start/end times; alarm test record; detector malfunction response log; entry permit showing monitoring requirement; post-entry equipment inspection record

controls.controlName: Mechanical Ventilation Sufficient to Maintain Safe Atmosphere
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work Planner and Ventilation Specialist (certified) | What: Establish mechanical ventilation with capacity verified by engineering calculation to maintain oxygen and remove contaminants; ensure ventilation remains operational throughout entry | When: Activated before entry and maintained continuously until last entrant exits and space is closed | Where: Space ventilation inlets and outlets; documented on work plan and permit | Why: Continuously removes toxic vapors, oxygen-depleted air, and other contaminants; prevents accumulation of hazardous atmosphere; enables safe breathing without respiratory protection | How: Calculate required air changes per minute based on space volume and contaminant type; select ventilation equipment (blower, ducting, exhaust fan) to meet capacity; position inlet to provide air coverage; establish continuous monitoring to verify flow; include ventilation failure alarm; document baseline airflow rate | Evidence: Engineering calculation showing ventilation capacity required and selected equipment specification; commissioning report verifying airflow rate; ventilation equipment inspection record; permit notation of ventilation requirement and verification

controls.controlName: Pre-Incident Rescue Plan Development and Testing
controls.controlStatement: Who: Permit Requestor with emergency response team and rescue personnel | What: Develop detailed rescue plan for each confined space entry scenario; conduct tabletop and full-scale drills before entry to ensure team readiness and correct procedures | When: Minimum 2 weeks before entry; drills completed before first entry | Where: Rescue plan document attached to permit; drill location at actual confined space or mock-up | Why: Ensures rescue team can respond quickly if entrant becomes incapacitated; verifies all equipment and procedures are in place; identifies procedure gaps or resource shortfalls before incident occurs | How: Identify potential emergency scenarios from hazard assessment (gas ingress, entrant collapse, tool drop); plan response including entry method, rescue sequence, equipment needs, personnel roles, communication protocol; conduct tabletop discussion with team to verify understanding; conduct field drill using rescue equipment and practicing entrant extraction; document any issues found and correct before live entry | Evidence: Completed rescue plan document with scenarios, resources, and procedures; drill attendance record with names and competencies; drill report identifying any findings or corrective actions; Field Manager approval signature on plan

controls.controlName: Continuous Entry Attendant Surveillance of Confined Space Entrants
controls.controlStatement: Who: Confined Space Entry Attendant (trained and competent) | What: Remain stationed outside space throughout entry; maintain continuous visual and audio contact with entrants; immediately raise alarm and initiate rescue if entrant becomes unresponsive or workspace conditions change | When: Entire duration of entry until last entrant exits | Where: Station positioned outside space with clear line of sight to entry point and audio communication with entrants | Why: Provides rapid detection of entrant distress or workplace hazard changes; initiates emergency response immediately without delay; prevents delay in rescue due to entrant inability to call for help | How: Position attendant where they can see entry point and all entrant movements; use communication equipment (radio, hand signals, rope signals) to maintain contact; observe for signs of distress (erratic movement, sudden silence, collapse); maintain alertness throughout entry (no other tasks); brief entrants on communication protocol and distress signals before entry | Evidence: Entry permit showing Attendant name and competency verification; communication system test log; post-entry Attendant statement confirming continuous monitoring; incident response drills showing Attendant initiation of emergency protocol

controls.controlName: Competency Assessment and Certification of Confined Space Roles
controls.controlStatement: Who: Site Training Coordinator and Specialist Signatory/Supervisor for Confined Space | What: Conduct skills assessment of all personnel in confined space roles (entrant, attendant, AGT, rescue team) per FLBM or contract requirements; issue competency certification; maintain training records | When: Before first assignment in role; refresher training every 24 months | Where: Training records file; competency matrix on display; permit system linked to competency database | Why: Ensures personnel understand confined space hazards and control procedures; prevents untrained personnel from assuming critical roles; enables permit issuer to verify team qualifications before authorizing entry | How: Conduct practical skills assessment including equipment use, procedure knowledge, hazard recognition, emergency response; document pass/fail result; issue competency certificate with scope and expiration date; post names of competent personnel; track refresher training dates; update permit system with current competencies | Evidence: Signed competency assessment records; competency certificates or cards held by personnel; training attendance records; current competency matrix posted; permit system showing verified competencies

controls.subProcess: Start Work Checks for High-Risk Activities
controls.controlName: Application of Start Work Checks for RAM Severity 4+ Activities
controls.controlStatement: Who: SWC Verifier (trained, competent person from work party not performing critical task) | What: Complete Start Work Check checklist for applicable high-risk activity (confined space, hot work, energy isolation, excavation, work at height, etc.) and verify all controls are physically in place before work commences | When: Immediately before starting work; at point of risk; re-verify after lunch, shift changes, or scope changes | Where: At equipment/work location; SWC checklist displayed at job site | Why: Ensures all control safeguards identified in planning are actually in place before exposing personnel to hazard; prevents work start with missing or degraded controls; provides second-pair-of-eyes verification | How: Obtain applicable IOGP 459-1 or Shell-branded SWC for activity; review checklist items with work party; visually verify each control is present and functional; mark Go/No-Go on checklist; if No-Go, stop work and escalate; if Go, proceed with work; require verifier signature | Evidence: Completed SWC checklist with date, work location, activity description, verifier name and signature; Go/No-Go decision clearly marked; incident logs showing SWC completed before all RAM Severity 4+ activities

controls.subProcess: Start Work Checks for High-Risk Activities
controls.controlName: SWC Verifier Competency and Authorization
controls.controlStatement: Who: Line Manager or Site Safety Coordinator | What: Identify and authorize competent personnel to serve as SWC Verifiers; confirm they understand the SWC process and can independently verify control placement | When: Before assigning as verifier; refresher confirmation annually | Where: Authorization list maintained and available to permit issuers | Why: Ensures only qualified personnel conduct verification; prevents errors in control assessment that could allow unsafe work to proceed | How: Select verifier from work party who is not performing critical task; confirm competency in the specific task type; provide SWC training if needed; authorize in writing; brief on process (visual verification, Go/No-Go decision, escalation if issues found); include verifier in toolbox talks | Evidence: Written authorization document for each verifier; SWC training records; competency confirmation; verifier availability list; post-job feedback from work crews on verifier effectiveness

controls.controlName: Structured Step-7 Conversation Before Work Start
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work party leader or any team member (rotated for engagement) | What: Conduct structured conversation with work party covering: (1) what is the work, (2) what could go wrong, (3) what barriers are in place, (4) what changes since planning, (5) contingencies if barrier fails | When: Immediately before work commences for RAM Severity 4+ activities | Where: At work location with tools and equipment visible; documented in toolbox talk record or permit conversation log | Why: Embeds human performance principles; ensures all team members understand hazards and controls; creates psychological safety for raising concerns; verifies barrier understanding at point of risk | How: Use Step-7 Conversation Card as guide; pose 'What' questions to team; encourage all participants to speak; discuss worst-case scenarios and response; identify any concerns or control gaps; address gaps before work start; document conversation summary | Evidence: Completed toolbox talk sheet or permit conversation log; attendance record with names; documented concerns raised and resolution; supervisor notes on conversation quality and engagement

controls.controlName: Span of Control and Competent Supervisor Assignment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Operations Manager or Site Superintendent | What: Define maximum span of control for supervisors based on activity risk and volume; assign only competent supervisors; ensure supervisor workload allows effective daily oversight of work activities | When: Before work begins; reviewed quarterly or when work volume changes | Where: Supervision plan document; organizational chart showing supervisor-to-worker ratios | Why: Prevents supervisor overload that reduces effectiveness in hazard monitoring; ensures supervisors have sufficient knowledge of activities being supervised; enables daily coaching and intervention before incidents | How: Assess number and risk level of permitted and non-permitted activities in supervisor's span; factor in supervisor's technical knowledge and coaching capability; limit span to manageable number (typically 5-8 teams depending on complexity); assign backup supervisor for coverage gaps; conduct supervisor competency assessments; rotate supervisors to develop bench strength | Evidence: Documented supervision plan with span of control ratios; supervisor competency matrix; workload tracking log; supervisor feedback on ability to provide effective oversight; incident trend analysis

controls.controlName: Active Supervision and Real-Time Coaching During Work Execution
controls.controlStatement: Who: Assigned Supervisor | What: Conduct real-time observation of work activities at the point of risk; provide coaching and feedback on safe practice compliance; intervene immediately if unsafe behavior observed | When: Continuously during work execution; minimum hourly presence on site for non-permitted work, more frequent for permitted high-risk work | Where: Work location; documented in supervision log or incident reports | Why: Detects unsafe practices or control breakdowns in real-time before injury occurs; coaches frontline to reinforce safe behaviors; demonstrates management commitment to safety | How: Use scheduled site walks/tours; position self where work is visible; ask workers about hazards and controls; recognize good safety practices; coach on deviations without blame; escalate systemic issues to operations; document coaching interactions | Evidence: Supervision observation logs with dates, activities, findings, coaching feedback; photographic/video evidence of hazard observations; coaching conversation records; corrective action follow-up documentation

controls.controlName: Procedure Development with Life-Critical Step Highlighting
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Owner and Work Planner | What: Develop clear procedures for routine and non-permitted work activities that identify life-critical steps upfront; describe tasks from safe-to-unsafe-to-safe; include hazard context and control rationale | When: Before authorizing routine work; reviewed and updated when process changes or incidents occur | Where: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) or Work Instruction document; linked from Permit Vision for non-permitted work | Why: Ensures consistent execution of critical control steps; prevents omission of safety-critical activities due to lack of knowledge or time pressure; provides basis for training and competency assessment | How: Conduct process mapping with frontline personnel; identify hazards using HEMP process; highlight life-critical control steps at start of procedure and at appropriate execution step; describe expected outcome and boundaries; include pictograms for visual clarity; field-test with typical users; obtain feedback and refine; link to training requirements | Evidence: Documented SOP with life-critical steps clearly marked; procedure version control with update history; field-testing feedback form; competency assessment results showing frontline understand critical steps

controls.controlName: Procedure Compliance Monitoring and Continuous Improvement
controls.controlStatement: Who: Supervisor and First-Line Assurance Team | What: Monitor adherence to procedures through observation and audits; solicit feedback from frontline on procedure usability; update procedures based on incidents, field learning, or regulatory changes | When: Ongoing monitoring during work execution; formal procedure review annually or after incidents | Where: Supervision logs, audit reports, lesson learned documents | Why: Identifies gaps between written procedure and actual work practice; reveals if procedures are too complex or unclear; ensures procedures stay current with process changes | How: Conduct spot-checks during work observation; review procedure usage during toolbox talks; perform quarterly procedure audits; document deviations from procedure and investigate root cause; collect frontline suggestions for improvement; update procedure when changes identified; communicate updates through toolbox talks and refresher training | Evidence: Procedure observation audit logs; deviation investigation reports; frontline feedback forms; updated procedure with change justification; training attendance for procedure updates

controls.subProcess: Personal Protective Equipment Selection and Use
controls.controlName: Risk-Based PPE Selection and Work Planning
controls.controlStatement: Who: Risk Assessment Team and Work Planner | What: Conduct exposure assessment and select PPE only after eliminating/substituting hazards; specify PPE type and use requirements on work permit or instruction; prioritize engineering controls over PPE | When: During work planning phase before work authorization; re-assess if work scope or hazard level changes | Where: Work permit or work instruction showing PPE hierarchy and selection rationale | Why: Ensures PPE is appropriate for actual hazard level; prevents over-reliance on PPE when engineering controls would be more effective; reduces unnecessary burden on workers | How: Apply Hierarchy of Controls (eliminate > substitute > engineer > administrative > PPE); justify why each higher control tier is not feasible; specify PPE type, size, condition requirements; include fit-testing or inspection procedures; consider PPE limitations for specific work (e.g., chemical suits limit mobility in confined space); field-verify correct type and fit before work start | Evidence: Completed exposure assessment; work permit showing PPE justification and hierarchy decision; PPE fit-testing records; supervisor pre-work PPE inspection checklist; post-work PPE inspection for damage

controls.subProcess: Personal Protective Equipment Selection and Use
controls.controlName: PPE Inspection, Maintenance, and Replacement Cycle
controls.controlStatement: Who: Site PPE Manager and Supervisor | What: Establish inventory of site PPE; inspect for damage before each use; replace damaged/expired items; document maintenance and replacement history | When: Pre-use inspection before each work activity; maintenance/repair cycle per manufacturer specifications; replacement per expiration date or damage discovery | Where: PPE storage area; inventory and maintenance log | Why: Prevents use of defective PPE that would fail to protect worker; ensures compliance with manufacturer specifications and regulatory requirements; tracks PPE lifecycle and budget planning | How: Create inventory list of all required PPE types and quantities; label items with inspection date; conduct visual inspection for tears, cracks, discoloration, damage; remove damaged items from service immediately; send for repair or dispose per procedure; replace expired items (note expiration dates on inventory); document all maintenance, repair, and replacement actions with date and reason | Evidence: PPE inventory list with quantities and inspection status; maintenance and replacement log with dates and signatures; photo documentation of damaged PPE; supplier certificates for new PPE showing specification compliance

controls.controlName: Appointment and Assessment of Electrical Safety Subject Matter Expert
controls.controlStatement: Who: Site Leadership or Project Manager with electrical competency validation committee | What: Assess and appoint qualified Electrical Safety SME; document appointment letter with roles, responsibilities, and scope limitations; maintain central registry of appointed SMEs | When: Upon asset start-up or when SME vacancy occurs; documented within 30 days; reviewed annually | Where: Appointment letter on file; Expertise Finders database or equivalent centralized listing | Why: Ensures clear authority and accountability for electrical safety governance; prevents conflicting electrical decisions by establishing single authority | How: Nominate candidate with demonstrated electrical expertise and SEAM knowledge; assess against SME competency criteria (technical knowledge, regulatory awareness, audit capability); conduct formal assessment by Electrical PTE or Safety CoE; issue appointment letter documenting scope and limitations; list in central database; schedule annual competency review | Evidence: Signed SME appointment letter with documented competencies; central registry entry; annual competency review record; decision rationale document

controls.controlName: Development and Implementation of Local Electrical Safety Rules
controls.controlStatement: Who: Electrical Safety SME in collaboration with operations and maintenance leads | What: Develop comprehensive local Electrical Safety Rules (ESR) document covering roles, competencies, safe work practices, isolation procedures, permits, and inspection requirements; publish and communicate to all personnel; update when regulatory requirements or site conditions change | When: Within 90 days of SME appointment; reviewed annually; updated when hazards or equipment changes | Where: Published in HSSE management system; accessible in training center and work planning office | Why: Provides clear, site-specific guidance on electrical hazard management; ensures consistent application of industry standards; serves as foundation for electrical competency and permits | How: Review industry standards (NFPA 70E, EN 50110-1); assess site electrical systems and hazards; draft ESR covering safe isolation, earthing, competency levels, PPE, permit requirements, emergency response; obtain regulatory and operational input; pilot with team; publish and train all personnel; maintain version control | Evidence: Published ESR document with version date and SME signature; training attendance records; regulatory compliance checklist; annual review with update log

controls.controlName: De-Energization and Lockout-Tagout of Electrical Equipment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Authorized Electrical Person (certified per ESR) | What: Isolate electrical equipment from all energy sources; apply lockout-tagout devices; verify isolation confirmed; document on authorized energization form before work begins | When: Before any work on de-energized electrical equipment; isolation verified and locked before workers approach equipment | Where: At isolation point on equipment; documented on work permit | Why: Prevents unexpected re-energization that would cause electrocution; lockout ensures isolation point cannot be accidentally closed while workers present | How: Identify all power sources for equipment (main feed, backup, stored energy); open disconnect switch or circuit breaker; apply personal safety padlock and danger tag with worker name; apply at all isolation points if multiple feeds; verify isolation with test equipment (multimeter or voltage tester); confirm zero voltage on all phases and ground | Evidence: Completed electrical safety lockout certificate with date, isolation points listed, worker signature; voltage test record; photographic proof of lockout installation; work permit annotation

controls.controlName: Voltage Verification and Earthing-Grounding of De-Energized Circuit
controls.controlStatement: Who: Authorized Electrical Person or assigned Test Technician | What: Verify absence of voltage on all phases and ground using calibrated test equipment; apply earthing/grounding cables to all circuit conductors to prevent stored charge release; install ground near work location | When: After isolation installed and before work begins; test repeated if power supply suspected changed during work | Where: At circuit breaker/disconnect and at work location; documented on permit | Why: Confirms that de-energization was successful and no live voltage remains; earthing prevents electric shock from stored charge in capacitors or inductors | How: Use calibrated voltage tester (multimeter or voltage detector); test on known live circuit to verify tester function; test all phases of isolated circuit; test between phase and ground; test between phases; after confirming zero voltage, install temporary earthing cables from each conductor to ground at work location; ensure earthing cable connections are secure; document test results and test instrument calibration date | Evidence: Voltage test record showing all phases tested and zero reading; test equipment calibration certificate; earthing cable installation photo; work permit signed off confirming zero-energy condition established

controls.controlName: Minimum Approach Distance Compliance for Overhead Power Lines
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work Supervisor and SWC Verifier | What: Verify minimum approach distance (MAD) from overhead power lines is maintained during work; install barriers or de-energize lines if MAD cannot be maintained; document distance requirement on work permit | When: Before work begins near overhead lines; continuously monitored during work | Where: Work location; documented on permit and in site safety plan | Why: Prevents arc flash or electrocution from voltage jumping across gap to equipment or personnel; arc can bridge distance greater than direct contact distance | How: Identify voltage of overhead line from utility; determine required MAD from standard table (e.g., NFPA 70E or local standard); measure distance from equipment/work location to line conductors; if MAD cannot be maintained, either de-energize and ground line (preferred) or install insulating barriers; brief work crew on danger zone; post warning signage; verify compliance before equipment operation or personnel entry | Evidence: Overhead line voltage identification record; MAD calculation with regulatory standard reference; distance measurement photo with scale; barrier installation photo; work permit showing MAD requirement; crew briefing attendance record

controls.controlName: Arc Flash PPE Selection and Use Based on Incident Energy
controls.controlStatement: Who: Electrical Safety SME or authorized arc flash assessment specialist | What: Conduct arc flash hazard analysis for energized work; calculate incident energy; select arc-rated PPE with appropriate thermal protection value; ensure PPE is worn correctly during work | When: Before any work near arc flash boundary; analysis updated when equipment changes | Where: Arc flash hazard label on equipment; documented in electrical maintenance manual | Why: Arc flash can cause severe burns to large body surface area in fractions of second; appropriate PPE rating reduces burn severity | How: Obtain equipment single-line diagram and nameplate data; use IEEE 1584 method or software tool to calculate incident energy at work distance; determine arc flash boundary; select arc-rated clothing, face shield, and gloves with ATPV rating equal to or greater than calculated incident energy; label equipment with arc flash hazard warning and PPE requirements; brief workers on PPE requirements; inspect PPE before use for tears or damage | Evidence: Arc flash hazard analysis report with incident energy calculations; equipment labeling with hazard warning; PPE specification and ATPV rating document; worker training record on PPE requirements; pre-work PPE inspection log

controls.controlName: Hot Work Permit System Implementation and Enforcement
controls.controlStatement: Who: Permit Requestor and Permit Issuer | What: Obtain hot work permit before beginning any hot work activity; permit documents hazards, controls, inspection requirements, and approvals; post permit visibly at work site | When: Before hot work start time; permit valid only for specified date/time/location; not valid for next day without re-issue | Where: Posted at work site; copy retained with work records | Why: Ensures hazard assessment completed, isolation confirmed, fire watch assigned, and environmental conditions verified before hot work commences; permit serves as final verification barrier | How: Initiate permit request minimum 24 hours before work; identify equipment type and material (thickness, substance); list all isolations and blinds required; specify fire watch requirements and hot work surveillance radius; specify wind speed and moisture limits; obtain PTW system approval after verifying all controls in place; sign and issue permit; post at site; collect completed permit documenting inspections and sign-offs after work concludes | Evidence: Completed hot work permit with all sections filled; regulatory required signatures present; environmental condition measurements recorded on permit; fire watch assignment documented; closure signature confirming safe site condition post-work

controls.controlName: Elimination of Flammable Atmosphere and Material Removal
controls.controlStatement: Who: Hot Work Supervisor and Area Safety Officer | What: Verify equipment interior is free of flammables, residue, and combustibles; confirm exterior surfaces within 10-meter radius cleared of flammable materials; document clearance on hot work permit | When: Minimum 2 hours before hot work commences; re-inspect if weather or conditions change | Where: Equipment interior and surrounding 10-meter work radius; documented on permit inspection checklist | Why: Prevents ignition of flammable vapors inside equipment or burning of nearby materials; fire spread to adjacent equipment or structures | How: Drain all flammable liquids from equipment; inspect interior for residue using borescope if applicable; pressure test or nitrogen purge equipment if required; blow out loose materials and debris; scan surrounding area within 10-meter radius; remove or cover flammable materials (wood, fabric, pipe insulation); relocate fuel containers minimum 10 meters away and upwind; verify no hot surfaces or ignition sources outside equipment that could ignite nearby materials | Evidence: Hot work permit with completed clearance checklist; supervisor sign-off confirming interior and exterior clear; photographic documentation of cleared area; weather observation log if moisture/wind is factor

controls.subProcess: Hot Work Supervision and Fire Watch
controls.controlName: Continuous Fire Watch During and After Hot Work
controls.controlStatement: Who: Assigned Fire Watch (competent personnel trained in hot work fire response) | What: Remain at hot work location throughout work and for minimum 30 minutes after completion; continuously observe work area and surroundings for fire signs; immediately call for evacuation and activate fire emergency response if fire detected | When: Entire duration of hot work; minimum 30-minute post-work monitoring | Where: Position with clear view of work area and surrounding radius | Why: Detects early ignition signs (smoke, flames, heat buildup) when fire is still controllable; rapid response prevents fire from spreading | How: Assign trained fire watch who is not performing hot work; position fire watch where work and surroundings are clearly visible; equip fire watch with suitable fire extinguisher (10lb minimum foam or CO2); brief fire watch on what to watch for (discoloration, smoke, flame, heat); maintain continuous observation (no breaks or distractions); after work ends, continue observation for 30 minutes; document fire watch end time on permit | Evidence: Hot work permit showing fire watch assignment and trained personnel name; fire watch briefing documentation; fire extinguisher inspection record; post-work sign-off by fire watch on permit confirming no fires detected

controls.controlName: Pre-Lift Risk Assessment and Lifting Plan Development
controls.controlStatement: Who: Lifting Supervisor or Competent Lift Planner | What: Conduct detailed risk assessment of planned lift; produce written lifting plan covering load weight, equipment specifications, personnel roles, communication procedure, environmental hazards, and contingency measures; obtain approvals before commencing | When: Minimum 5 working days before lift (complex lift 10+ days); plan finalized 24 hours before execution | Where: Lifting Plan document; stored with Lifting Supervisor | Why: Ensures all hazards identified, controls specified, and team prepared; prevents missed scenarios or under-estimated loads | How: Establish lift working group with crane operator, rigger, lifting supervisor, and subject matter experts; determine actual load weight with tolerance; select crane and rigging equipment with appropriate SWL (Safe Working Load); calculate hook load including attachments; identify environmental hazards (wind, overhead obstacles, ground conditions); specify rigging configuration and sling angles; establish communication protocol (radio frequencies, hand signals); document contingencies (bad weather delay, load entanglement); walk down lift route; obtain sign-offs from all stakeholders | Evidence: Formal Lifting Plan document with all sections completed; load calculation sheet with engineer stamp; equipment specification and SWL certification; lift route diagram; team approval signatures; contingency plan

controls.controlName: Pre-Lift Meeting and Go/No-Go Decision
controls.controlStatement: Who: Lifting Supervisor with all lift team members (operator, rigger, signal person, ground crew) | What: Conduct face-to-face meeting minimum 30 minutes before lift; review lifting plan, roles, communication method, environmental conditions; conduct site walk-down; make explicit Go/No-Go decision before commencing | When: Day of lift, minimum 30 minutes before scheduled lift time | Where: At lift site with full view of equipment and load area | Why: Ensures all team members understand plan and potential problems; detects last-minute hazards or equipment issues before lift starts; confirms team readiness and communication | How: Gather full lift team; distribute and review lifting plan; walk the route confirming no obstructions, soft ground, or hazards; inspect lifting equipment (crane, slings, shackles) for damage; verify radio communication works; confirm weather conditions acceptable; ask each team member if they identify any concerns; address all concerns before proceeding; make formal Go/No-Go decision with clear announcement; if No-Go, document reason and re-schedule | Evidence: Pre-lift meeting sign-in sheet with all participants; documented Go/No-Go decision with date/time; equipment inspection checklist; weather observation record; any No-Go reasons documented with resolution plan

controls.controlName: Load Control and Real-Time Communication During Lift
controls.controlStatement: Who: Crane Operator with Lifting Supervisor and dedicated Signal Person | What: Execute lift following plan; maintain two-way radio contact throughout; signal person confirms load control at each step; operator stops immediately if any unsafe condition observed; load held no longer than necessary | When: Continuous during lift from first hook engagement until load safely set | Where: All participants at designated communication distance and positions | Why: Permits immediate halt if load tilts, sling fails, or obstacle encountered; prevents load drop or uncontrolled movement | How: Operator verifies load hooked and rigging secure before lift begins; signal person maintains visual contact with load and gives only authorized commands (lift/lower/hold); lifting supervisor observes from overall vantage point; if any concern observed, lifting supervisor immediately commands operator to hold; operator confirms hold status; team assesses situation and determines if safe to continue; communication continues until load reaches ground and slings released; record any holds or deviations on lift report | Evidence: Radio communication log documenting key commands and confirmations; lift execution report noting any holds or deviations; post-lift team debrief notes; incident log (if applicable) with response actions

controls.controlName: Underground Services Localization Before Excavation
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work Planner and Excavation Supervisor (with utility locating specialist if contracted) | What: Locate and mark all underground utilities (electrical, gas, water, communications) before any excavation begins; verify locations with utility operators; document marked locations on excavation plan | When: Minimum 3 working days before excavation; re-verify if excavation scope changes | Where: Marked on ground with paint/flags; documented on excavation permit and site plan | Why: Prevents striking buried electrical lines (electrocution), gas lines (explosion), or water lines (operational disruption); identifies hazards for excavation equipment operators and workers | How: Request utility locating via call-before-you-dig system (e.g., national dial service); provide excavation area boundaries and depth; utilities mark with paint and flags; verify marking with utility operators; photograph marked locations; transfer marks to excavation permit with coordinates and utility type; identify any utilities not located (dry lines, private utilities) and plan hand-digging verification; brief equipment operators on marked locations | Evidence: Call-before-you-dig confirmation receipt; photographic record of marked utilities; utility operator verification signature; excavation permit showing utility locations; hand-digging protocol if unlocated utilities suspected

controls.controlName: Ground Support and Slope Management During Excavation
controls.controlStatement: Who: Competent Excavation Supervisor (trained in soil classification and slope stability) | What: Determine soil type and classify per regulatory standard; specify protective system (sloped walls, shored walls, or trench boxes); verify system installed correctly; inspect before personnel entry | When: Before excavation begins; daily visual inspection during work; re-inspect after rain or significant digging | Where: Excavation walls and floor; documented on excavation permit | Why: Prevents trench collapse that would bury or crush workers; ensures selected protection system is appropriate for soil conditions | How: Conduct soil test boring or visual soil assessment to determine soil type and stability; consult excavation engineer to determine required slope angle or support system; if soil is unstable, select shoring system (hydraulic shores, sheet piling, or trench box); oversee installation and verification before workers enter; daily walk-around inspection for wall integrity, water seepage, or ground movement; if wall cracks appear, evacuate and reassess | Evidence: Soil classification report; excavation engineer design specification; support system installation photos; daily inspection logs with supervisor signature; any modifications documented with engineer re-approval
keyActivities: Classify soil type through testing or visual assessment
Determine required slope angle per soil classification
Select protective system (sloped wall, shored, or trench box)
Verify system installation before worker entry
Conduct daily wall integrity inspections
Monitor for water seepage in trench
Halt work and evacuate if cracks or movement observed
controls.riskAddressed: Trench collapse buries or crushes workers causing severe injury or death; occurs suddenly without warning if support inadequate
controls.testingApproach: Review soil classification and engineer specification; verify support system installed per design; observe daily inspection practices; interview supervisor on instability signs

controls.controlName: Work Scheduling to Prevent Fatigue Accumulation
controls.controlStatement: Who: Operations Manager and Shift Scheduler | What: Establish work schedules limiting consecutive hours worked and consecutive days on duty; ensure minimum rest periods between shifts; assess roster monthly for fatigue risk accumulation | When: Schedules reviewed monthly; fatigue risk assessment quarterly; adjusted if risk indicators elevated | Where: Published shift schedule; fatigue risk register | Why: Chronic fatigue impairs cognition and reaction time increasing error and incident risk; prevents fatigue-related incidents | How: Establish policy limiting consecutive hours per shift (typically 10-12 hours) and consecutive on-duty days (max 7); require minimum rest period between shifts (typically 10-12 hours); analyze roster for fatigue risk factors; adjust schedules when risk present; communicate with sufficient notice | Evidence: Published work schedule; fatigue risk assessment document; scheduling policy document; incident analysis showing fatigue involvement; worker feedback on schedule adequacy
keyActivities: Develop fatigue-aware scheduling policy
Limit consecutive hours per shift
Limit consecutive days on duty before rest
Ensure minimum rest hours between shifts
Assess monthly schedules for fatigue risk patterns
Modify schedules to reduce cumulative fatigue
Monitor incident data for fatigue involvement
controls.riskAddressed: Chronic fatigue impairs decision-making and reaction time increasing operational errors; fatigue-related incidents have higher severity
controls.testingApproach: Review shift schedules for policy compliance; assess fatigue risk patterns in roster; interview workers on fatigue; examine incident reports for fatigue contribution
